Huffman coding provides marginal gain (e.g., 32x to 40x) compared to pruning and quantization.
Weight sharing across layers not explored; each layer quantized independently.
papers.absEn: Neural networks are both computationally intensive and memory intensive, making them difficult to deploy on embedded systems with limited hardware resources. To address this limitation, we introduce "deep compression", a three stage pipeline: pruning, trained quantization and Huffman coding, that work together to reduce the storage requirement of neural networks by 35x to 49x without affecting their accuracy. Our method first prunes the network by learning only the important connections. Next, we quantize the weights to enforce weight sharing, finally, we apply Huffman coding. After the first two steps we retrain the network to fine tune the remaining connections and the quantized centroids. Pruning, reduces the number of connections by 9x to 13x; Quantization then reduces the number of bits that represent each connection from 32 to 5. On the ImageNet dataset, our method reduced the storage required by AlexNet by 35x, from 240MB to 6.9MB, without loss of accuracy. Our method reduced the size of VGG-16 by 49x from 552MB to 11.3MB, again with no loss of accuracy. This allows fitting the model into on-chip SRAM cache rather than off-chip DRAM memory. Our compression method also facilitates the use of complex neural networks in mobile applications where application size and download bandwidth are constrained. Benchmarked on CPU, GPU and mobile GPU, compressed network has 3x to 4x layerwise speedup and 3x to 7x better energy efficiency.

papers.tEn: Deep Reinforcement Learning with Double Q-learning
papers.tZh: 深度强化学习中的双Q学习
papers.sEn: Double Q-learning reduces overestimations in DQN, improving performance on Atari games.
contrib: Shows Q-learning overestimates action values in large-scale deterministic problems due to inherent estimation errors.
Demonstrates DQN suffers substantial overestimations in Atari 2600 games, harming performance.
Generalizes Double Q-learning to large-scale function approximation with deep neural networks.
Proposes Double DQN algorithm, reducing overestimations and improving policy quality.
Achieves state-of-the-art results on Atari 2600 domain with Double DQN.
limits: Evaluation limited to deterministic Atari 2600 games; stochastic environments not tested.
Hyperparameters tuned for DQN, not optimized for Double DQN in main comparison.
Double DQN may not eliminate overestimations entirely; residual bias possible.
Theoretical analysis assumes unbiased estimates; practical violations may affect guarantees.
Results may not generalize to other domains beyond Atari 2600.
papers.absEn: The popular Q-learning algorithm is known to overestimate action values under certain conditions. It was not previously known whether, in practice, such overestimations are common, whether they harm performance, and whether they can generally be prevented. In this paper, we answer all these questions affirmatively. In particular, we first show that the recent DQN algorithm, which combines Q-learning with a deep neural network, suffers from substantial overestimations in some games in the Atari 2600 domain. We then show that the idea behind the Double Q-learning algorithm, which was introduced in a tabular setting, can be generalized to work with large-scale function approximation. We propose a specific adaptation to the DQN algorithm and show that the resulting algorithm not only reduces the observed overestimations, as hypothesized, but that this also leads to much better performance on several games.

papers.tEn: Continuous control with deep reinforcement learning
papers.tZh: 连续控制与深度强化学习
papers.sEn: We adapt Deep Q-Learning to continuous action domains using an actor-critic, model-free algorithm based on deterministic policy gradients, achieving competitive performance on over 20 simulated physics tasks.
contrib: Adapts Deep Q-Learning to continuous action domains via deterministic policy gradient.
Proposes Deep DPG (DDPG) algorithm with actor-critic, replay buffer, and target networks.
Solves over 20 simulated physics tasks with same hyper-parameters and network architecture.
Achieves competitive performance with planning algorithms that have full dynamics access.
Demonstrates end-to-end learning from raw pixel inputs for many tasks.
Combines batch normalization for stable learning across tasks with different units.
limits: Requires large number of training episodes, limiting sample efficiency.
No convergence guarantees due to non-linear function approximators.
Exploration noise must be tuned per environment for effective exploration.
Performance degrades on harder tasks with inaccurate Q-value estimates.
Limited to simulated environments; real-world applicability not tested.
papers.absEn: We adapt the ideas underlying the success of Deep Q-Learning to the continuous action domain. We present an actor-critic, model-free algorithm based on the deterministic policy gradient that can operate over continuous action spaces. Using the same learning algorithm, network architecture and hyper-parameters, our algorithm robustly solves more than 20 simulated physics tasks, including classic problems such as cartpole swing-up, dexterous manipulation, legged locomotion and car driving. Our algorithm is able to find policies whose performance is competitive with those found by a planning algorithm with full access to the dynamics of the domain and its derivatives. We further demonstrate that for many of the tasks the algorithm can learn policies end-to-end: directly from raw pixel inputs.

papers.tEn: You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection
papers.tZh: 你只需看一眼：统一、实时的目标检测
papers.sEn: YOLO frames object detection as a regression problem, enabling real-time processing with a single neural network.
contrib: Frames object detection as a single regression problem from image pixels to bounding boxes and class probabilities.
Unified architecture enables end-to-end training and real-time speeds at 45 fps for YOLO and 155 fps for Fast YOLO.
YOLO reasons globally about the image, reducing background false positives by half compared to Fast R-CNN.
Learns generalizable object representations, outperforming DPM and R-CNN when generalizing from natural images to artwork.
Combining YOLO with Fast R-CNN boosts mAP by 3.2% on VOC 2007 by reducing background errors.
Open-source framework with pretrained models, enabling easy reproduction and deployment.
limits: YOLO makes more localization errors than state-of-the-art detectors, especially for small objects.
Spatial constraints limit the number of nearby objects YOLO can predict, struggling with groups of small objects.
Loss function treats errors equally for large and small boxes, not aligning perfectly with detection performance.
Coarse features from downsampling layers hinder precise bounding box prediction.
YOLO lags behind state-of-the-art in accuracy on standard benchmarks like VOC 2012.
papers.absEn: We present YOLO, a new approach to object detection. Prior work on object detection repurposes classifiers to perform detection. Instead, we frame object detection as a regression problem to spatially separated bounding boxes and associated class probabilities. A single neural network predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities directly from full images in one evaluation. Since the whole detection pipeline is a single network, it can be optimized end-to-end directly on detection performance. Our unified architecture is extremely fast. Our base YOLO model processes images in real-time at 45 frames per second. A smaller version of the network, Fast YOLO, processes an astounding 155 frames per second while still achieving double the mAP of other real-time detectors. Compared to state-of-the-art detection systems, YOLO makes more localization errors but is far less likely to predict false detections where nothing exists. Finally, YOLO learns very general representations of objects. It outperforms all other detection methods, including DPM and R-CNN, by a wide margin when generalizing from natural images to artwork on both the Picasso Dataset and the People-Art Dataset.

papers.tEn: Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with Region Proposal Networks
papers.tZh: Faster R-CNN: 基于区域提议网络的实时目标检测
papers.sEn: Faster R-CNN introduces a Region Proposal Network that shares convolutional features with the detection network, enabling nearly cost-free region proposals and real-time object detection.
contrib: Introduces Region Proposal Network (RPN) sharing convolutional features with detection network, enabling nearly cost-free region proposals.
Proposes anchor boxes as regression references for multi-scale and aspect ratio prediction without image or filter pyramids.
Develops a 4-step alternating training algorithm to share convolutional layers between RPN and Fast R-CNN, forming a unified network.
Achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on PASCAL VOC 2007, 2012, and MS COCO with only 300 proposals per image.
Demonstrates near real-time performance at 5fps on GPU with VGG-16, including all detection steps.
Serves as foundation for 1st-place entries in ILSVRC and COCO 2015 competitions across multiple tracks.
limits: RPN relies on pre-defined anchor scales and aspect ratios, which may not generalize to all object shapes.
Training requires careful handling of cross-boundary anchors to avoid convergence issues.
Non-maximum suppression is needed to reduce proposal redundancy, adding a post-processing step.
Performance depends on pre-trained ImageNet models, limiting applicability to domains with different data distributions.
Joint training of RPN and Fast R-CNN is approximate, ignoring gradients w.r.t. proposal box coordinates.
papers.absEn: State-of-the-art object detection networks depend on region proposal algorithms to hypothesize object locations. Advances like SPPnet and Fast R-CNN have reduced the running time of these detection networks, exposing region proposal computation as a bottleneck. In this work, we introduce a Region Proposal Network (RPN) that shares full-image convolutional features with the detection network, thus enabling nearly cost-free region proposals. An RPN is a fully convolutional network that simultaneously predicts object bounds and objectness scores at each position. The RPN is trained end-to-end to generate high-quality region proposals, which are used by Fast R-CNN for detection. We further merge RPN and Fast R-CNN into a single network by sharing their convolutional features---using the recently popular terminology of neural networks with 'attention' mechanisms, the RPN component tells the unified network where to look. For the very deep VGG-16 model, our detection system has a frame rate of 5fps (including all steps) on a GPU, while achieving state-of-the-art object detection accuracy on PASCAL VOC 2007, 2012, and MS COCO datasets with only 300 proposals per image. In ILSVRC and COCO 2015 competitions, Faster R-CNN and RPN are the foundations of the 1st-place winning entries in several tracks. Code has been made publicly available.

papers.tEn: Fast R-CNN
papers.tZh: Fast R-CNN
papers.sEn: Fast R-CNN achieves faster training and testing speeds with higher accuracy for object detection.
contrib: Proposes Fast R-CNN, a single-stage training algorithm for object detection that jointly learns classification and bounding-box regression.
Achieves 9x faster training and 213x faster testing than R-CNN with VGG16, while improving mAP on PASCAL VOC 2012.
Introduces RoI pooling layer for efficient feature sharing and back-propagation through region proposals.
Uses multi-task loss combining softmax classification and robust L1 bounding-box regression.
Demonstrates that fine-tuning convolutional layers is crucial for very deep networks like VGG16.
Shows that sparse object proposals improve detector quality, enabling efficient evaluation.
limits: Relies on pre-computed object proposals, which limits end-to-end training and may miss objects.
Single-scale training and testing may not generalize well to extreme scale variations.
GPU memory constraints limit the use of very deep networks with multi-scale training.
Performance degrades when using dense object proposals instead of sparse ones.
Method is evaluated only on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO datasets; generalization to other domains is not tested.
papers.absEn: This paper proposes a Fast Region-based Convolutional Network method (Fast R-CNN) for object detection. Fast R-CNN builds on previous work to efficiently classify object proposals using deep convolutional networks. Compared to previous work, Fast R-CNN employs several innovations to improve training and testing speed while also increasing detection accuracy. Fast R-CNN trains the very deep VGG16 network 9x faster than R-CNN, is 213x faster at test-time, and achieves a higher mAP on PASCAL VOC 2012. Compared to SPPnet, Fast R-CNN trains VGG16 3x faster, tests 10x faster, and is more accurate. Fast R-CNN is implemented in Python and C++ (using Caffe) and is available under the open-source MIT License at https://github.com/rbgirshick/fast-rcnn.

papers.tEn: End-to-End Training of Deep Visuomotor Policies
papers.tZh: 深度视觉运动策略的端到端训练
papers.sEn: Jointly training perception and control end-to-end yields better performance than separate training for visuomotor policies.
contrib: Develops a guided policy search method that transforms policy search into supervised learning using trajectory-centric RL.
Introduces a novel CNN architecture with spatial soft-argmax for end-to-end visuomotor policy learning from raw images to torques.
Demonstrates end-to-end training of perception and control outperforms separate training on real-world manipulation tasks.
Achieves sample-efficient learning with only tens of minutes of real-world interaction time for complex 7-DoF tasks.
Provides simulated comparisons showing guided policy search outperforms prior methods like REPS, RWR, and PILCO.
Formalizes guided policy search as BADMM, ensuring convergence to a locally optimal solution.
limits: Requires full state observability during training, limiting applicability to instrumented setups.
Assumes low-noise dynamics for linear-Gaussian trajectory optimization, which may not hold in highly stochastic environments.
Uses first-moment constraints on actions, which may be insufficient for tasks requiring precise higher-order statistics.
Pretraining requires ImageNet initialization and pose regression data, adding dependency on external datasets.
Policy architecture with spatial soft-argmax is specialized for visuomotor tasks and may not generalize to other perception problems.
papers.absEn: Policy search methods can allow robots to learn control policies for a wide range of tasks, but practical applications of policy search often require hand-engineered components for perception, state estimation, and low-level control. In this paper, we aim to answer the following question: does training the perception and control systems jointly end-to-end provide better performance than training each component separately? To this end, we develop a method that can be used to learn policies that map raw image observations directly to torques at the robot's motors. The policies are represented by deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with 92,000 parameters, and are trained using a partially observed guided policy search method, which transforms policy search into supervised learning, with supervision provided by a simple trajectory-centric reinforcement learning method. We evaluate our method on a range of real-world manipulation tasks that require close coordination between vision and control, such as screwing a cap onto a bottle, and present simulated comparisons to a range of prior policy search methods.

papers.tEn: Deep Unsupervised Learning using Nonequilibrium Thermodynamics
papers.tZh: 基于非平衡热力学的深度无监督学习
papers.sEn: A novel generative model inspired by non-equilibrium statistical physics that learns to reverse a diffusion process to restore data structure.
contrib: Introduces diffusion probabilistic models inspired by non-equilibrium statistical physics, achieving both flexibility and tractability.
Defines a forward diffusion process that gradually destroys data structure and learns a reverse process to restore it.
Enables exact sampling, log-likelihood evaluation, and multiplication with other distributions for posterior computation.
Demonstrates high log-likelihood on diverse datasets including MNIST, CIFAR-10, and dead leaves images.
Provides entropy bounds for the reverse diffusion process and a lower bound on log-likelihood for training.
Releases open-source reference implementation for reproducibility.
limits: Requires many diffusion steps (thousands) for high-quality samples, increasing computational cost.
Performance depends on the choice of diffusion schedule and noise type (Gaussian or binomial).
The method assumes smooth target distributions; highly multimodal or discontinuous distributions may be challenging.
Training requires careful tuning of hyperparameters like step size and network architecture.
The lower bound on log-likelihood may be loose for finite diffusion steps, affecting model evaluation.
papers.absEn: A central problem in machine learning involves modeling complex data-sets using highly flexible families of probability distributions in which learning, sampling, inference, and evaluation are still analytically or computationally tractable. Here, we develop an approach that simultaneously achieves both flexibility and tractability. The essential idea, inspired by non-equilibrium statistical physics, is to systematically and slowly destroy structure in a data distribution through an iterative forward diffusion process. We then learn a reverse diffusion process that restores structure in data, yielding a highly flexible and tractable generative model of the data. This approach allows us to rapidly learn, sample from, and evaluate probabilities in deep generative models with thousands of layers or time steps, as well as to compute conditional and posterior probabilities under the learned model. We additionally release an open source reference implementation of the algorithm.

papers.tEn: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
papers.tZh: 神经网络中的知识蒸馏
papers.sEn: Distilling knowledge from an ensemble into a single model improves performance and efficiency.
contrib: Introduces knowledge distillation, transferring ensemble knowledge into a single small model using soft targets with temperature scaling.
Shows distilled model on MNIST achieves 74 errors vs 146 for a regularized small net, even without seeing digit 3 in transfer set.
Demonstrates distillation improves commercial speech recognition: distilled single model matches ensemble performance with 10.9% WER.
Proposes specialist ensemble: one generalist plus many specialists for fine-grained classes, trained in parallel, improving JFT accuracy by 4.4%.
Shows soft targets prevent overfitting with limited data: 3% of speech data yields 57% frame accuracy vs 44.5% with hard targets.
Provides theoretical analysis: high-temperature distillation approximates minimizing squared logit difference.
limits: Distillation requires a pre-trained cumbersome model, which may be computationally expensive to obtain.
Optimal temperature is task-dependent and requires tuning; low temperatures may ignore useful negative logits.
Specialist ensemble relies on clustering of confusable classes, which may not be optimal for all datasets.
Distillation performance degrades when distilled model is too small to capture all knowledge from cumbersome model.
Method assumes soft targets from cumbersome model are reliable; noisy or biased cumbersome models may transfer errors.
papers.absEn: A very simple way to improve the performance of almost any machine learning algorithm is to train many different models on the same data and then to average their predictions. Unfortunately, making predictions using a whole ensemble of models is cumbersome and may be too computationally expensive to allow deployment to a large number of users, especially if the individual models are large neural nets. Caruana and his collaborators have shown that it is possible to compress the knowledge in an ensemble into a single model which is much easier to deploy and we develop this approach further using a different compression technique. We achieve some surprising results on MNIST and we show that we can significantly improve the acoustic model of a heavily used commercial system by distilling the knowledge in an ensemble of models into a single model. We also introduce a new type of ensemble composed of one or more full models and many specialist models which learn to distinguish fine-grained classes that the full models confuse. Unlike a mixture of experts, these specialist models can be trained rapidly and in parallel.

papers.tEn: Trust Region Policy Optimization
papers.tZh: 信任区域策略优化
papers.sEn: TRPO is a practical algorithm for optimizing policies with guaranteed monotonic improvement, effective for large nonlinear policies like neural networks.
contrib: Proves a monotonic improvement guarantee for policy optimization with KL divergence penalty.
Develops TRPO, a practical algorithm using a KL divergence constraint for robust updates.
Demonstrates TRPO's effectiveness on simulated robotic locomotion and Atari games.
Unifies policy gradient and policy iteration methods under a trust region framework.
Achieves state-of-the-art performance on high-dimensional control tasks with neural network policies.
limits: Assumes exact advantage evaluation, which is unrealistic in practice.
Vine method requires state resets, limiting applicability to simulation.
Theoretical bound uses maximum KL divergence, but practical algorithm uses average KL.
Sample complexity may be high for real-world robotic tasks due to on-policy sampling.
Performance on Atari games is not consistently superior to specialized methods like DQN.
papers.absEn: We describe an iterative procedure for optimizing policies, with guaranteed monotonic improvement. By making several approximations to the theoretically-justified procedure, we develop a practical algorithm, called Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO). This algorithm is similar to natural policy gradient methods and is effective for optimizing large nonlinear policies such as neural networks. Our experiments demonstrate its robust performance on a wide variety of tasks: learning simulated robotic swimming, hopping, and walking gaits; and playing Atari games using images of the screen as input. Despite its approximations that deviate from the theory, TRPO tends to give monotonic improvement, with little tuning of hyperparameters.

papers.tEn: Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift
papers.tZh: 批量归一化：通过减少内部协变量偏移加速深度网络训练
papers.sEn: Batch Normalization allows higher learning rates, reduces need for Dropout, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with fewer training steps.
contrib: Proposes Batch Normalization to reduce internal covariate shift, enabling higher learning rates and faster training.
Normalizes layer inputs using mini-batch statistics, making normalization part of the model architecture.
Allows use of saturating nonlinearities by preventing saturation, and acts as a regularizer reducing need for Dropout.
Achieves same accuracy with 14x fewer training steps on ImageNet, and sets new state-of-the-art with 4.9% top-5 error.
Introduces learned affine parameters to restore network representation capacity after normalization.
Demonstrates that Batch Normalization improves gradient flow and stabilizes parameter growth.
limits: Normalization depends on mini-batch size; very small batches may lead to inaccurate statistics.
The method assumes that layer inputs have Gaussian-like distribution; non-Gaussian inputs may reduce effectiveness.
Batch Normalization adds computational overhead during training due to per-batch statistics computation.
The theoretical understanding of why Batch Normalization works remains incomplete, especially for gradient propagation.
The method may not generalize well to recurrent networks or tasks with very small batch sizes.
papers.absEn: Training Deep Neural Networks is complicated by the fact that the distribution of each layer's inputs changes during training, as the parameters of the previous layers change. This slows down the training by requiring lower learning rates and careful parameter initialization, and makes it notoriously hard to train models with saturating nonlinearities. We refer to this phenomenon as internal covariate shift, and address the problem by normalizing layer inputs. Our method draws its strength from making normalization a part of the model architecture and performing the normalization for each training mini-batch. Batch Normalization allows us to use much higher learning rates and be less careful about initialization. It also acts as a regularizer, in some cases eliminating the need for Dropout. Applied to a state-of-the-art image classification model, Batch Normalization achieves the same accuracy with 14 times fewer training steps, and beats the original model by a significant margin. Using an ensemble of batch-normalized networks, we improve upon the best published result on ImageNet classification: reaching 4.9% top-5 validation error (and 4.8% test error), exceeding the accuracy of human raters.

papers.tEn: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
papers.tZh: Adam: 一种随机优化方法
papers.sEn: Adam is an efficient stochastic optimization method that uses adaptive moment estimates.
contrib: Introduces Adam, an efficient first-order gradient-based optimization algorithm.
Uses adaptive estimates of lower-order moments for parameter updates.
Invariant to diagonal rescaling of gradients, suitable for large-scale problems.
Handles non-stationary objectives and noisy/sparse gradients effectively.
Provides theoretical convergence analysis with a regret bound comparable to best known results.
Empirically outperforms other stochastic optimization methods on various tasks.
limits: Convergence analysis assumes convex objectives, not guaranteed for non-convex problems.
Hyper-parameters may require tuning for specific applications despite intuitive interpretation.
Memory requirements scale with number of parameters due to moment storage.
Performance may degrade on very high-dimensional or ill-conditioned problems.
papers.absEn: We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments. The method is straightforward to implement, is computationally efficient, has little memory requirements, is invariant to diagonal rescaling of the gradients, and is well suited for problems that are large in terms of data and/or parameters. The method is also appropriate for non-stationary objectives and problems with very noisy and/or sparse gradients. The hyper-parameters have intuitive interpretations and typically require little tuning. Some connections to related algorithms, on which Adam was inspired, are discussed. We also analyze the theoretical convergence properties of the algorithm and provide a regret bound on the convergence rate that is comparable to the best known results under the online convex optimization framework. Empirical results demonstrate that Adam works well in practice and compares favorably to other stochastic optimization methods. Finally, we discuss AdaMax, a variant of Adam based on the infinity norm.

papers.tEn: Going Deeper with Convolutions
papers.tZh: 深入卷积网络
papers.sEn: We propose Inception, a deep CNN architecture that achieves state-of-the-art results in ILSVRC 2014 by efficiently using computing resources to increase depth and width.
contrib: Proposed the Inception architecture that improves computing resource utilization by increasing depth and width while keeping computational budget constant.
Introduced dimension reduction using 1x1 convolutions to remove computational bottlenecks.
Achieved state-of-the-art results on ILSVRC 2014 classification and detection with GoogLeNet.
Demonstrated that approximating optimal sparse structure with dense components is viable for improving neural networks.
Showed that increasing network depth and width with efficient design can improve accuracy without proportional computational cost.
Used auxiliary classifiers to encourage discrimination in lower stages and provide regularization.
limits: The Inception architecture was designed specifically for computer vision tasks and may not generalize to other domains.
The architecture relies on manual design and tuning; automated construction of similar topologies remains future work.
The computational efficiency gains are achieved at the cost of increased architectural complexity.
The results are based on a specific competition dataset (ILSVRC 2014) and may not fully represent real-world performance.
The detection pipeline did not use bounding box regression or context, which could further improve accuracy.
papers.absEn: We propose a deep convolutional neural network architecture codenamed "Inception", which was responsible for setting the new state of the art for classification and detection in the ImageNet Large-Scale Visual Recognition Challenge 2014 (ILSVRC 2014). The main hallmark of this architecture is the improved utilization of the computing resources inside the network. This was achieved by a carefully crafted design that allows for increasing the depth and width of the network while keeping the computational budget constant. To optimize quality, the architectural decisions were based on the Hebbian principle and the intuition of multi-scale processing. One particular incarnation used in our submission for ILSVRC 2014 is called GoogLeNet, a 22 layers deep network, the quality of which is assessed in the context of classification and detection.

papers.tEn: Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks
papers.tZh: 基于神经网络的序列到序列学习
papers.sEn: A deep LSTM-based end-to-end approach achieves BLEU 34.8 on WMT'14 English-French translation, outperforming a phrase-based SMT system (33.3) and reaching 36.5 when reranking SMT hypotheses.
contrib: Proposes an end-to-end LSTM-based sequence-to-sequence model for general sequence learning.
Achieves BLEU score of 34.8 on WMT'14 English-French translation, outperforming phrase-based SMT baseline.
Reversing source sentences introduces short-term dependencies, improving LSTM performance on long sentences.
LSTM learns sentence representations sensitive to word order and invariant to active/passive voice.
Deep LSTMs with 4 layers significantly outperform shallow LSTMs.
LSTM rescoring of SMT n-best lists achieves BLEU 36.5, close to previous best result.
limits: Limited vocabulary (80k target words) penalizes BLEU for out-of-vocabulary words.
Requires large computational resources (8 GPUs, 10 days training).
Beam search decoder is approximate and may not find optimal translation.
Method assumes monotonic alignment between input and output sequences.
Performance on tasks with very limited training data is not evaluated.
papers.absEn: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are powerful models that have achieved excellent performance on difficult learning tasks. Although DNNs work well whenever large labeled training sets are available, they cannot be used to map sequences to sequences. In this paper, we present a general end-to-end approach to sequence learning that makes minimal assumptions on the sequence structure. Our method uses a multilayered Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) to map the input sequence to a vector of a fixed dimensionality, and then another deep LSTM to decode the target sequence from the vector. Our main result is that on an English to French translation task from the WMT'14 dataset, the translations produced by the LSTM achieve a BLEU score of 34.8 on the entire test set, where the LSTM's BLEU score was penalized on out-of-vocabulary words. Additionally, the LSTM did not have difficulty on long sentences. For comparison, a phrase-based SMT system achieves a BLEU score of 33.3 on the same dataset. When we used the LSTM to rerank the 1000 hypotheses produced by the aforementioned SMT system, its BLEU score increases to 36.5, which is close to the previous best result on this task. The LSTM also learned sensible phrase and sentence representations that are sensitive to word order and are relatively invariant to the active and the passive voice. Finally, we found that reversing the order of the words in all source sentences (but not target sentences) improved the LSTM's performance markedly, because doing so introduced many short term dependencies between the source and the target sentence which made the optimization problem easier.

papers.tEn: Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition
papers.tZh: 用于大规模图像识别的极深卷积网络
papers.sEn: Increasing depth with small 3x3 filters significantly improves accuracy, achieving 16-19 weight layers.
contrib: Systematic evaluation of increasing depth in ConvNets using very small 3x3 filters.
Demonstrated that pushing depth to 16-19 weight layers significantly improves accuracy.
Achieved first and second places in ILSVRC 2014 localization and classification tracks.
Showed that learned representations generalize well to other datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results.
Publicly released two best-performing ConvNet models to facilitate further research.
Confirmed that depth is crucial for visual representation quality.
limits: Evaluation limited to ImageNet dataset; generalization to other domains not fully explored.
Depth beyond 19 layers did not improve performance; saturation observed.
Training required significant computational resources (2-3 weeks on 4 GPUs).
Architecture uses only 3x3 filters; other filter sizes not systematically compared.
Multi-crop evaluation increases computation time without proportional accuracy gain.
papers.absEn: In this work we investigate the effect of the convolutional network depth on its accuracy in the large-scale image recognition setting. Our main contribution is a thorough evaluation of networks of increasing depth using an architecture with very small (3x3) convolution filters, which shows that a significant improvement on the prior-art configurations can be achieved by pushing the depth to 16-19 weight layers. These findings were the basis of our ImageNet Challenge 2014 submission, where our team secured the first and the second places in the localisation and classification tracks respectively. We also show that our representations generalise well to other datasets, where they achieve state-of-the-art results. We have made our two best-performing ConvNet models publicly available to facilitate further research on the use of deep visual representations in computer vision.

papers.tEn: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
papers.tZh: 通过联合学习对齐和翻译的神经机器翻译
papers.sEn: Neural machine translation can be improved by allowing the model to automatically search for relevant parts of the source sentence without encoding it into a fixed-length vector.
contrib: Proposes an attention mechanism that allows soft-search over source sentence parts for each target word.
Eliminates the fixed-length vector bottleneck in encoder-decoder models, improving long sentence translation.
Achieves translation performance comparable to phrase-based SMT on English-to-French WMT'14.
Uses bidirectional RNN encoder to capture both preceding and following context for each word.
Jointly trains alignment and translation in a single neural network via backpropagation.
Visualizes soft-alignments that align with linguistic intuition, handling non-monotonic reordering.
limits: Computes annotation weights for every source word per target word, limiting scalability to very long sequences.
Requires fixed vocabulary with unknown word token, failing to handle rare or out-of-vocabulary words.
Tested only on English-to-French; effectiveness on other language pairs with different reordering patterns is unverified.
Soft alignment is computationally expensive compared to hard alignment used in traditional SMT.
Performance relies on large parallel corpora; may degrade with limited data.
papers.absEn: Neural machine translation is a recently proposed approach to machine translation. Unlike the traditional statistical machine translation, the neural machine translation aims at building a single neural network that can be jointly tuned to maximize the translation performance. The models proposed recently for neural machine translation often belong to a family of encoder-decoders and consists of an encoder that encodes a source sentence into a fixed-length vector from which a decoder generates a translation. In this paper, we conjecture that the use of a fixed-length vector is a bottleneck in improving the performance of this basic encoder-decoder architecture, and propose to extend this by allowing a model to automatically (soft-)search for parts of a source sentence that are relevant to predicting a target word, without having to form these parts as a hard segment explicitly. With this new approach, we achieve a translation performance comparable to the existing state-of-the-art phrase-based system on the task of English-to-French translation. Furthermore, qualitative analysis reveals that the (soft-)alignments found by the model agree well with our intuition.

papers.tEn: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
papers.tZh: ImageNet大规模视觉识别挑战赛
papers.sEn: The ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge is a benchmark for object category classification and detection, driving advances in computer vision.
contrib: Introduced the ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC), a benchmark for object classification and detection with 1000 categories and millions of images.
Developed scalable crowdsourcing methods for large-scale image annotation, achieving 99.7% precision.
Demonstrated that deep convolutional neural networks (e.g., AlexNet) significantly outperform traditional methods, marking a turning point in object recognition.
Provided a detailed analysis of algorithmic progress over five years, comparing state-of-the-art computer vision accuracy with human accuracy.
Established evaluation metrics for large-scale classification, localization, and detection, including top-5 error and average precision.
Released a publicly available dataset and competition framework that became the standard benchmark for object recognition research.
limits: Dataset annotation only labels one object category per image for classification, causing ambiguity when multiple objects are present.
Evaluation metrics for localization may be inaccurate for images with clustered objects, requiring manual removal of 3.5% of images.
Small object instances pose challenges for localization evaluation; threshold adjustment is needed for objects under 25x25 pixels.
The dataset's 1000 categories are limited to ImageNet synsets and may not cover all real-world object types.
Crowdsourcing annotation quality relies on worker consensus, which may vary across different object categories.
papers.absEn: The ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge is a benchmark in object category classification and detection on hundreds of object categories and millions of images. The challenge has been run annually from 2010 to present, attracting participation from more than fifty institutions. This paper describes the creation of this benchmark dataset and the advances in object recognition that have been possible as a result. We discuss the challenges of collecting large-scale ground truth annotation, highlight key breakthroughs in categorical object recognition, provide a detailed analysis of the current state of the field of large-scale image classification and object detection, and compare the state-of-the-art computer vision accuracy with human accuracy. We conclude with lessons learned in the five years of the challenge, and propose future directions and improvements.

papers.tEn: Generative Adversarial Networks
papers.tZh: 生成对抗网络
papers.sEn: We propose a new framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process, where a generative model and a discriminative model are trained simultaneously, with the generator aiming to fool the discriminator.
contrib: Proposes adversarial training framework with generator and discriminator networks.
Shows global optimum when generator matches data distribution, discriminator outputs 1/2.
Eliminates need for Markov chains or approximate inference during training or sampling.
Demonstrates competitive sample quality on MNIST, TFD, and CIFAR-10.
Introduces minimax game formulation for generative model estimation.
limits: No explicit representation of probability density p_g(x).
Training requires careful synchronization between G and D to avoid mode collapse.
Theoretical guarantees assume infinite capacity; practical MLPs may have critical points.
Likelihood estimation via Parzen window has high variance and poor high-dimensional performance.
Framework limited to differentiable generator and discriminator functions.
papers.absEn: We propose a new framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process, in which we simultaneously train two models: a generative model G that captures the data distribution, and a discriminative model D that estimates the probability that a sample came from the training data rather than G. The training procedure for G is to maximize the probability of D making a mistake. This framework corresponds to a minimax two-player game. In the space of arbitrary functions G and D, a unique solution exists, with G recovering the training data distribution and D equal to 1/2 everywhere. In the case where G and D are defined by multilayer perceptrons, the entire system can be trained with backpropagation. There is no need for any Markov chains or unrolled approximate inference networks during either training or generation of samples. Experiments demonstrate the potential of the framework through qualitative and quantitative evaluation of the generated samples.

papers.tEn: Spectral Networks and Locally Connected Networks on Graphs
papers.tZh: 谱网络与图上的局部连接网络
papers.sEn: We propose two generalizations of CNNs to signals on general domains: one via hierarchical clustering and another via graph Laplacian spectrum, enabling efficient deep architectures with input-size-independent parameters for low-dimensional graphs.
contrib: Proposes two generalizations of CNNs to graph-structured data: spatial and spectral constructions.
Shows that spatial construction achieves O(n) parameters using hierarchical clustering and local receptive fields.
Introduces spectral construction using graph Laplacian eigenvectors, achieving O(n) parameters per feature map.
Demonstrates O(1) parameter construction via smooth spectral multipliers, enabling parameter count independent of input size.
Validates both constructions on low-dimensional graph datasets, showing improved efficiency over fully connected networks.
Shows that smooth spectral constraints yield spatially localized filters that generalize across graph locations.
limits: Spatial construction lacks weight sharing across different graph locations.
Spectral construction requires expensive multiplication by eigenvectors and their transpose.
High-frequency eigenvectors may be noisy; spectral construction may not capture meaningful information from them.
Naive multiscale clustering used in spatial construction does not guarantee compatibility with graph Laplacian.
Experiments limited to low-dimensional graphs; scalability to high-dimensional or large-scale graphs not fully explored.
papers.absEn: Convolutional Neural Networks are extremely efficient architectures in image and audio recognition tasks, thanks to their ability to exploit the local translational invariance of signal classes over their domain. In this paper we consider possible generalizations of CNNs to signals defined on more general domains without the action of a translation group. In particular, we propose two constructions, one based upon a hierarchical clustering of the domain, and another based on the spectrum of the graph Laplacian. We show through experiments that for low-dimensional graphs it is possible to learn convolutional layers with a number of parameters independent of the input size, resulting in efficient deep architectures.

papers.tEn: Rich feature hierarchies for accurate object detection and semantic segmentation
papers.tZh: 用于精确目标检测和语义分割的丰富特征层次结构
papers.sEn: R-CNN combines region proposals with CNNs to achieve a 30% relative improvement in mAP on PASCAL VOC 2012.
contrib: Proposes R-CNN, combining region proposals with CNNs for object detection.
Achieves 30% relative mAP improvement on PASCAL VOC 2012 over prior best.
Shows supervised pre-training on auxiliary task plus domain-specific fine-tuning boosts performance.
Demonstrates R-CNN outperforms OverFeat on ILSVRC2013 detection by large margin.
Extends R-CNN to semantic segmentation with competitive results on PASCAL VOC.
Provides analysis of error modes and bounding-box regression to reduce mislocalizations.
limits: Requires fixed-size CNN input via warping, which may distort aspect ratios.
Region proposal stage (selective search) is a bottleneck for speed and recall.
Fine-tuning and SVM training use different positive/negative definitions, causing inconsistency.
Bounding-box regression only effective for proposals near ground truth, limiting scope.
Performance depends heavily on pre-training dataset (ImageNet) and architecture choice.
papers.absEn: Object detection performance, as measured on the canonical PASCAL VOC dataset, has plateaued in the last few years. The best-performing methods are complex ensemble systems that typically combine multiple low-level image features with high-level context. In this paper, we propose a simple and scalable detection algorithm that improves mean average precision (mAP) by more than 30% relative to the previous best result on VOC 2012---achieving a mAP of 53.3%. Our approach combines two key insights: (1) one can apply high-capacity convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to bottom-up region proposals in order to localize and segment objects and (2) when labeled training data is scarce, supervised pre-training for an auxiliary task, followed by domain-specific fine-tuning, yields a significant performance boost. Since we combine region proposals with CNNs, we call our method R-CNN: Regions with CNN features. We also compare R-CNN to OverFeat, a recently proposed sliding-window detector based on a similar CNN architecture. We find that R-CNN outperforms OverFeat by a large margin on the 200-class ILSVRC2013 detection dataset. Source code for the complete system is available at http://www.cs.berkeley.edu/~rbg/rcnn.

papers.tEn: Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality
papers.tZh: 词汇与短语的分布式表示及其组合性
papers.sEn: We extend the Skip-gram model with subsampling and negative sampling to learn high-quality word and phrase vectors efficiently.
contrib: Introduced negative sampling as a simple alternative to hierarchical softmax for efficient training.
Demonstrated that subsampling frequent words speeds up training and improves vector quality for rare words.
Extended Skip-gram to learn phrase vectors by treating phrases as single tokens via data-driven phrase detection.
Showed that word vectors exhibit linear structure enabling analogical reasoning via vector arithmetic.
Demonstrated additive compositionality of word vectors, e.g., vec('Russia') + vec('river') ≈ vec('Volga River').
Trained high-quality vectors on billions of words, significantly outperforming prior models on analogy tasks.
limits: Word representations are indifferent to word order, limiting capture of syntactic structure.
Inability to represent idiomatic phrases as compositions of individual word meanings.
Negative sampling does not maximize log probability of softmax, only approximates it.
Phrase detection threshold and subsampling rate are heuristic and task-dependent.
Model performance heavily depends on large training data and careful hyperparameter tuning.
papers.absEn: The recently introduced continuous Skip-gram model is an efficient method for learning high-quality distributed vector representations that capture a large number of precise syntactic and semantic word relationships. In this paper we present several extensions that improve both the quality of the vectors and the training speed. By subsampling of the frequent words we obtain significant speedup and also learn more regular word representations. We also describe a simple alternative to the hierarchical softmax called negative sampling. An inherent limitation of word representations is their indifference to word order and their inability to represent idiomatic phrases. For example, the meanings of "Canada" and "Air" cannot be easily combined to obtain "Air Canada". Motivated by this example, we present a simple method for finding phrases in text, and show that learning good vector representations for millions of phrases is possible.

papers.tEn: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
papers.tZh: 词向量空间中的高效估计方法
papers.sEn: Proposed two novel model architectures for computing continuous word vectors from large datasets, achieving higher accuracy with lower computational cost.
contrib: Proposes CBOW and Skip-gram models for efficient learning of high-quality word vectors from large data.
Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on syntactic and semantic word similarity tasks with low computational cost.
Demonstrates that word vectors capture linear regularities, enabling algebraic operations like vector("King") - vector("Man") + vector("Woman") ≈ vector("Queen").
Introduces a comprehensive test set with 8869 semantic and 10675 syntactic questions for evaluating word vectors.
Shows that increasing both vector dimensionality and training data size yields diminishing returns, requiring balanced scaling.
Achieves new state-of-the-art on Microsoft Sentence Completion Challenge (58.9% accuracy) by combining Skip-gram with RNNLM.
limits: Models treat words as atomic units, ignoring morphology; synonyms are counted as mistakes in evaluation.
Performance saturates with increasing vector dimensions or data size, requiring joint scaling for improvement.
Skip-gram model underperforms on syntactic tasks compared to CBOW, limiting its general applicability.
Training on limited in-domain data (e.g., speech recognition) may not benefit from simple scaling, requiring advanced techniques.
Evaluation metric (cosine distance) may not capture all semantic nuances, and 100% accuracy is likely unattainable.
papers.absEn: We propose two novel model architectures for computing continuous vector representations of words from very large data sets. The quality of these representations is measured in a word similarity task, and the results are compared to the previously best performing techniques based on different types of neural networks. We observe large improvements in accuracy at much lower computational cost, i.e. it takes less than a day to learn high quality word vectors from a 1.6 billion words data set. Furthermore, we show that these vectors provide state-of-the-art performance on our test set for measuring syntactic and semantic word similarities.

papers.tEn: Improving neural networks by preventing co-adaptation of feature detectors
papers.tZh: 通过防止特征检测器的共适应来改进神经网络
papers.sEn: Randomly omitting half of the feature detectors during training prevents co-adaptation and reduces overfitting, leading to significant improvements in neural network performance.
contrib: Proposes dropout, a technique that randomly omits half of feature detectors during training to prevent co-adaptation.
Shows dropout reduces overfitting significantly on small training sets.
Demonstrates dropout as efficient model averaging, approximating exponentially many networks.
Achieves state-of-the-art results on speech (TIMIT) and object recognition (CIFAR-10, ImageNet) benchmarks.
Introduces L2 weight constraints per hidden unit to allow large learning rates and thorough weight-space search.
Connects dropout to evolutionary theory, suggesting robustness through multiple alternative feature sets.
limits: Dropout probability of 0.5 is used throughout; optimal probability may vary per layer or dataset.
Method primarily tested on feedforward neural networks; applicability to recurrent or other architectures not explored.
Performance gains depend on network size and training data amount; may diminish with very large datasets.
Test-time mean network approximation assumes equal importance of all dropout networks; may not hold in all cases.
Dropout increases training time due to stochastic updates and requires careful tuning of learning rate and constraints.
papers.absEn: When a large feedforward neural network is trained on a small training set, it typically performs poorly on held-out test data. This "overfitting" is greatly reduced by randomly omitting half of the feature detectors on each training case. This prevents complex co-adaptations in which a feature detector is only helpful in the context of several other specific feature detectors. Instead, each neuron learns to detect a feature that is generally helpful for producing the correct answer given the combinatorially large variety of internal contexts in which it must operate. Random "dropout" gives big improvements on many benchmark tasks and sets new records for speech and object recognition.
